explanation:
Svelte is a compiler — it transforms your components into optimized vanilla JavaScript at build time, with no runtime framework. Reactivity is built into the language: assigning to a variable triggers UI updates. The `$:` label marks reactive declarations that re-run whenever their dependencies change, creating derived values without explicit subscriptions.

This is fundamentally different from React (manual `useState`/`useEffect`) or Vue (ref/reactive wrappers). In Svelte, `let count = 0; count++` updates the DOM automatically. The `$:` syntax creates computed values that stay in sync — like spreadsheet formulas that recalculate when inputs change.
key_takeaway: Svelte reactivity is assignment-based — `let x = 0; x++` updates the DOM. Use `$:` for derived values. Arrays and objects must be reassigned (not mutated) to trigger updates.
try_it: Build a temperature converter with two inputs (Celsius and Fahrenheit). Use `$:` reactive declarations so changing one automatically updates the other.

explanation:
Svelte components are single `.svelte` files containing `<script>`, HTML markup, and optional `<style>`. Props are declared with `export let` — the exported variable becomes an attribute on the component element. Default values are assigned directly: `export let color = 'blue'` makes the prop optional.

Events use `createEventDispatcher` to emit custom events from child to parent. Slots let parent components pass content into children — like React's `children` but with named slots for multiple insertion points. Component composition in Svelte feels like writing plain HTML with superpowers.
key_takeaway: Props are `export let` variables. Events use `createEventDispatcher`. Slots pass content from parent to child — named slots for multiple insertion points, default slot for body content.
try_it: Create a `Modal` component with a `title` prop, a default slot for body content, a named `footer` slot for action buttons, and an `on:close` event.

explanation:
Svelte stores are reactive containers for shared state. A `writable` store holds a value that any component can read or update. A `readable` store is read-only (useful for WebSocket data, timers). A `derived` store computes values from other stores. Components subscribe to stores with the `$` prefix — `$count` auto-subscribes and unsubscribes.

Stores solve cross-component state management without prop drilling. Unlike React Context (re-renders all consumers), Svelte stores update only the specific `$store` references in templates. The `$` syntax is syntactic sugar for `subscribe`/`unsubscribe` — it handles cleanup automatically.
key_takeaway: `writable` stores for shared mutable state, `derived` for computed values, `readable` for external data. The `$` prefix auto-subscribes in templates — no manual cleanup needed.
try_it: Create a `settings` writable store with theme (light/dark), fontSize (12-24), and language. Add a `derived` store that computes CSS variables from the settings. Persist to localStorage.

explanation:
Svelte components have a lifecycle: they're created, mounted to the DOM, updated when state changes, and destroyed when removed. `onMount` runs after the component is first rendered to the DOM — this is where you fetch data, set up subscriptions, and initialize third-party libraries. `onDestroy` runs when the component is removed — clean up timers, event listeners, and subscriptions.

The `beforeUpdate` and `afterUpdate` hooks run before and after every DOM update, not just the first. They're useful for scroll position management and measuring DOM elements. `tick()` is a promise that resolves after the next DOM update — use it when you need to read updated DOM state.
key_takeaway: `onMount` runs after first render — return a cleanup function instead of using `onDestroy`. Use `tick()` to wait for DOM updates before reading element state.
try_it: Create a component that fetches paginated data on mount, implements infinite scrolling with `IntersectionObserver`, and cleans up the observer on destroy.

explanation:
SvelteKit is Svelte's full-stack framework with file-based routing, server-side rendering (SSR), and API routes. Pages live in `src/routes` — `src/routes/about/+page.svelte` maps to `/about`. Dynamic routes use brackets: `src/routes/users/[id]/+page.svelte` matches `/users/123`. A `+page.server.ts` file runs on the server to load data.

Layouts wrap groups of pages with shared UI (navigation, sidebars). `src/routes/+layout.svelte` applies to all pages; nested layouts in subdirectories compose. Server-side `load` functions run before rendering, fetch data, and pass it to the page as props. Form actions handle mutations (POST requests) with progressive enhancement.
key_takeaway: File-based routing maps directories to URLs. `+page.server.ts` loads data on the server. Layouts (`+layout.svelte`) wrap pages with shared UI. Form actions handle mutations with progressive enhancement.
try_it: Create a `/blog` route with an index page listing posts and a `[slug]` dynamic route for individual posts. Add a layout with a sidebar showing categories.

explanation:
Svelte has built-in transition directives that animate elements as they enter and leave the DOM. `transition:fade`, `transition:fly`, `transition:slide` — each takes parameters for duration, delay, and easing. Unlike CSS animations, Svelte transitions are bidirectional: the same animation plays in reverse when the element is removed.

Custom transitions are functions that return CSS or JavaScript-based animations. The `animate:` directive reorders elements in keyed each blocks. The `motion` module (`tweened`, `spring`) creates smooth value interpolations for numbers, colors, and objects.
key_takeaway: `transition:` animates enter/leave, `animate:flip` reorders list items smoothly, `spring` creates physics-based bouncy motion, and `tweened` creates smooth number interpolation.
try_it: Create a notification toast system where toasts slide in from the right, auto-dismiss after 3 seconds with a progress bar, and slide out when closed.

explanation:
Svelte practice problems focus on mastering reactivity primitives (reactive declarations, stores), component composition (props, events, slots), and SvelteKit's file-based routing with server-side load functions. The key pattern is assignment-based reactivity — you trigger updates by reassigning variables, not calling setter functions.

For animations and transitions, the pattern is declarative: attach `transition:` directives to elements and Svelte handles enter/leave animations automatically. Combined with `tweened` and `spring` for value interpolation, Svelte provides a complete animation toolkit without external libraries.
key_takeaway: Svelte reactivity is assignment-based. Use stores for shared state, transitions for declarative animations, and SvelteKit file-based routing for full-stack apps.
try_it: Complete all 5 practice problems — each tests a core Svelte/SvelteKit pattern.

practice_challenges.title: Reactive Temperature Converter
practice_challenges.hint: Use two-way binding with bind:value and reactive declarations ($:) to keep both inputs in sync.
practice_challenges.description: Build a temperature converter with Celsius and Fahrenheit inputs. Changing one should automatically update the other using Svelte reactive declarations.

practice_challenges.title: Multi-Step Form Wizard with Validation
practice_challenges.hint: Create a WizardStep component with slots, track the current step index, and use transition:fly for step transitions.
practice_challenges.description: Build a 3-step form wizard where each step slides in with a fly transition. Steps include Personal Info, Address, and Confirmation. Validate each step before proceeding.
practice_challenges.example_input: User fills step 1 (name, email), clicks Next
practice_challenges.example_output: Step 1 flies out left, step 2 flies in from right, progress bar updates to 66%

practice_challenges.title: SvelteKit Blog with Dynamic Routes
practice_challenges.hint: Use +page.server.ts for data loading, [slug] for dynamic routes, and +layout.svelte for shared navigation.
practice_challenges.description: Build a blog with an index page listing posts, a [slug] route for individual posts, and a layout with sidebar categories. Load data server-side.
practice_challenges.example_output: Page renders with full post content, sidebar shows categories, back link returns to index

practice_challenges.title: Custom Spring Physics Gallery
practice_challenges.hint: Use svelte/motion spring for drag position, transition:scale for image modals, and animate:flip for reordering.
practice_challenges.description: Build a photo gallery where images can be dragged with spring physics, clicked to open a scaled modal, and reordered with animated flipping.
practice_challenges.example_output: Images smoothly spring to new positions using spring physics, modal opens with scale transition on click

practice_challenges.title: Realtime Collaborative Whiteboard
practice_challenges.hint: Use writable store for drawing points, onMount for canvas setup, broadcast channel for multi-user sync, and tweened for smooth line interpolation.
practice_challenges.description: Build a collaborative whiteboard where multiple users can draw simultaneously. Use stores for state, canvas API for rendering, and Supabase broadcast channels for real-time sync.
practice_challenges.example_input: User A draws a circle, User B draws a square simultaneously
practice_challenges.example_output: Both drawings appear on both canvases in real-time with smooth interpolation

explanation:
Svelte challenges test your ability to build reactive UIs with minimal boilerplate. The key patterns are: Reactive declarations with $: syntax, Two-way binding with bind:, Component communication via props and events, and Stores for shared state.

Start with a simple reactive counter and progress to building a full app with stores, transitions, and component composition. The hardest challenges involve custom stores with derived state, complex transitions, and slot-based component APIs.
key_takeaway: Svelte reactivity is built into the language — use $: for derived values and stores for shared state instead of external state libraries.
try_it: Build a counter with increment/decrement buttons before attempting stores.

practice_challenges.title: Reactive Temperature Converter
practice_challenges.hint: Use bind:value for inputs and $: to derive the converted temperature.
practice_challenges.description: Build a temperature converter with two inputs (Celsius/Fahrenheit) that update each other reactively using $: declarations.

practice_challenges.title: Accordion Component
practice_challenges.hint: Use slide transition and a slot for the panel content. Track open state per panel.
practice_challenges.description: Create a reusable accordion component with smooth slide transitions and slot-based content panels.
practice_challenges.example_output: Click Q1, panel slides open showing A1

practice_challenges.title: Custom Store: Stopwatch
practice_challenges.hint: Implement the store contract (subscribe method). Use setInterval internally, return start/stop/reset.
practice_challenges.description: Build a custom Svelte store that implements a stopwatch with start, stop, reset, and lap tracking functionality.
practice_challenges.example_output: Store value updates from 0 to 5000 over 5 seconds

practice_challenges.title: Drag & Drop List
practice_challenges.hint: Use mousedown/mousemove/mouseup events. Apply flip animation with animate:flip for reordering.
practice_challenges.description: Implement a sortable list with drag-and-drop reordering, using Svelte's flip animations for smooth transitions.
practice_challenges.example_output: List reorders with smooth flip animation

practice_challenges.title: Reactive Form Validation
practice_challenges.hint: Create a form store that tracks dirty fields, validation errors, and submit state. Derive isValid reactively.
practice_challenges.description: Build a form system with reactive validation — fields validate on blur, errors display inline, submit is disabled until valid, with a custom useForm store.
practice_challenges.example_input: user types 'ab' in email field, tabs out
practice_challenges.example_output: Error: 'Invalid email' shown inline, form.submit disabled
